---
layout: post
title: "Absolute Value and the Real Line"
date: 2026-03-30
category: maths
lang: en
lang_pair: "/blog/absolute-value-and-the-real-line-hindi/"
permalink: /blog/absolute-value-and-the-real-line-english/
description: "Definition of absolute value, four key properties, Triangle Inequality, Reverse Triangle Inequality, and distance on the real line — with proofs and worked examples."
---

<style>
  .math-post{font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.9;color:#2d2d2d;max-width:860px;margin:0 auto}

  .post-hero::before{content:"|x|";position:absolute;right:12px;top:4px;font-size:6rem;color:rgba(255,255,255,0.07);font-family:Georgia,serif;font-style:italic}

  .rev-card.b{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa)}

  .app-card p{font-size:.85rem;color:#475569;margin:0;line-height:1.55}
  .app-icon{font-size:1.6rem}

  .summary-table th{background:#1e3c72;color:white;padding:11px 14px;text-align:left;font-size:.82rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em}

  .summary-table tr:nth-child(even) td{background:#f8fafc}

  .highlight-result p{margin:0;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.75}

  .social-footer p{margin:8px 0}
  .pdf-section{text-align:center;margin:44px 0 28px}
  .pdf-btn{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1B2A4A,#2A5298);color:white;padding:15px 36px;border-radius:6px;font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1rem;font-weight:700;box-shadow:0 4px 18px rgba(27,42,74,.40);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn:hover{transform:translateY(-3px);box-shadow:0 8px 26px rgba(27,42,74,.50);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn .pi{font-size:1.5rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt{display:flex;flex-direction:column;text-align:left;line-height:1.35}
  .pdf-btn .pt1{font-size:1rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt2{font-size:.72rem;opacity:.82;font-weight:400}

</style>

<div class="math-post">

<!-- §1 HERO + STATS -->
<div class="post-hero">
  <h1>Absolute Value and the Real Line</h1>
  <p>The absolute value $\lvert x \rvert$ is one of the most used tools in all of analysis — yet its power rests on just four simple properties. In this post we define it carefully from the ordered field structure of $\mathbb{R}$, prove all four properties by case analysis, derive the celebrated <strong>Triangle Inequality</strong> and its reverse form, and interpret $\lvert x - y \rvert$ as the distance between two points on the real line.</p>
  <a class="lang-switch" href="/blog/absolute-value-and-the-real-line-hindi/">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में पढ़ें</a>
</div>

<div class="stats-strip">
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Key properties</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">|x+y|</span><span class="stat-lbl">Triangle Inequality</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">d(x,y)</span><span class="stat-lbl">Distance on ℝ</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">1840s</span><span class="stat-lbl">Weierstrass notation</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">CSIR/GATE/JAM</span><span class="stat-lbl">Exam relevance</span></div>
</div>

<!-- §2 DEFINITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ede9fe;">|·|</div>
  <h2>Definition of Absolute Value</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Absolute Value — Rudin, Ch. 1, Definition 1.32</div>
  <p>For $x \in \mathbb{R}$, the <strong>absolute value</strong> of $x$ is:</p>
  <p>$$\lvert x \rvert = \begin{cases} x & \text{if } x \geq 0, \\ -x & \text{if } x < 0. \end{cases}$$</p>
  <p>Equivalently, $\lvert x \rvert = \max\{x, -x\}$.</p>
  <p><strong>Geometric meaning:</strong> $\lvert x \rvert$ is the <em>distance</em> of $x$ from $0$ on the real line. More generally, $\lvert x - y \rvert$ is the distance between $x$ and $y$: we write $d(x,y) = \lvert x - y \rvert$.</p>
</div>

<div class="numberline-wrap">
  <svg viewBox="0 0 500 80" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:480px;width:100%">
    <line x1="30" y1="40" x2="470" y2="40" stroke="#1e3c72" stroke-width="2"/>
    <polygon points="470,36 480,40 470,44" fill="#1e3c72"/>
    <line x1="250" y1="33" x2="250" y2="47" stroke="#1e3c72" stroke-width="2"/>
    <text x="248" y="62" font-size="13" fill="#1e3c72" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif">0</text>
    <line x1="350" y1="33" x2="350" y2="47" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="2"/>
    <text x="350" y="62" font-size="13" fill="#7c3aed" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif">x</text>
    <line x1="140" y1="33" x2="140" y2="47" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/>
    <text x="140" y="62" font-size="13" fill="#dc2626" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif">−x</text>
    <line x1="250" y1="30" x2="350" y2="30" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
    <text x="300" y="22" font-size="12" fill="#7c3aed" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif">|x|</text>
    <line x1="250" y1="18" x2="140" y2="18" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
    <text x="195" y="12" font-size="12" fill="#dc2626" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif">|x| = −x</text>
  </svg>
  <p style="font-size:.85rem;color:#64748b;margin:8px 0 0;">$\lvert x \rvert$ = distance from $x$ to $0$; &nbsp; $\lvert x - y \rvert$ = distance between $x$ and $y$</p>
</div>

> 📖 **Reference:** Rudin, W., *Principles of Mathematical Analysis*, 3rd ed., Ch. 1, Definition 1.32 and Theorem 1.33. Also: Apostol, T.M., *Mathematical Analysis*, 2nd ed., Ch. 1, §1.11–1.12.

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F517; Prerequisites</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/order-relations-and-ordered-sets-english/"><strong>Order Relations and Ordered Sets</strong></a> — $\lvert x \rvert$ is defined using the order on $\mathbb{R}$; sign of $x$ determines which case applies.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/consequences-of-field-axioms-english/"><strong>Consequences of Field Axioms</strong></a> — sign rules $(-a)(-b)=ab$ and $-(-a)=a$ are used in the proofs.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — case-split proofs and mathematical induction are the main techniques here.</p>
</div>

<!-- §3 INTUITION & HISTORY -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#d1fae5;">&#x1F4A1;</div>
  <h2>Properties, Intuition, and History</h2>
</div>

<p>The four properties of $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ can be remembered through the geometric picture: $\lvert x \rvert$ is a <strong>length</strong> (always non-negative), zero only when the point is at the origin, multiplication scales lengths, and the triangle inequality says the "straight-line" path is never longer than the two-step path through an intermediate point.</p>

<div class="prop-grid">
  <div class="prop-card" style="border-left-color:#059669;">
    <div class="prop-label" style="color:#059669;">(P1) Non-negativity</div>
    <div class="prop-formula">$\lvert x \rvert \geq 0$</div>
    <div class="prop-note">Lengths are never negative.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="prop-card" style="border-left-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="prop-label" style="color:#2a5298;">(P2) Definiteness</div>
    <div class="prop-formula">$\lvert x \rvert = 0 \Leftrightarrow x = 0$</div>
    <div class="prop-note">Only the origin has zero distance from itself.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="prop-card" style="border-left-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="prop-label" style="color:#7c3aed;">(P3) Multiplicativity</div>
    <div class="prop-formula">$\lvert xy \rvert = \lvert x \rvert \lvert y \rvert$</div>
    <div class="prop-note">Scaling is compatible with absolute value.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="prop-card" style="border-left-color:#dc2626;">
    <div class="prop-label" style="color:#dc2626;">(P4) Triangle Inequality</div>
    <div class="prop-formula">$\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$</div>
    <div class="prop-note">Direct path $\leq$ two-step path.</div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="info-card green">
  <div class="info-card-header green">&#x1F50D; The Triangle Inequality and Its Reverse</div>
  <p><strong>Triangle Inequality (P4):</strong> $\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$. <em>Proof:</em> Since $-\lvert x \rvert \leq x \leq \lvert x \rvert$ and $-\lvert y \rvert \leq y \leq \lvert y \rvert$, adding gives $-(\lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert) \leq x+y \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$, which is exactly $\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$.</p>
  <p><strong>Reverse Triangle Inequality:</strong> $\bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert \leq \lvert x - y \rvert$. <em>Proof:</em> Apply (P4) to $x = (x-y)+y$: $\lvert x \rvert \leq \lvert x-y \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$, so $\lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \leq \lvert x-y \rvert$. Swapping $x,y$: $\lvert y \rvert - \lvert x \rvert \leq \lvert x-y \rvert$. Together these give the result.</p>
  <p><strong>Key usage:</strong> (P4) gives an <em>upper</em> bound for $\lvert x+y \rvert$; the reverse form gives a <em>lower</em> bound for $\lvert x-y \rvert$. Both are essential in analysis proofs.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4DC; Historical Background &amp; Motivation</div>
  <p><strong>Origin Story.</strong> The vertical-bar notation $\lvert x \rvert$ was introduced by <strong>Karl Weierstrass</strong> in the 1840s as part of his systematic programme to arithmetise analysis — replacing geometric intuition with precise algebraic inequalities. <strong>Augustin-Louis Cauchy</strong> had used absolute values informally in his 1821 <em>Cours d'Analyse</em> to state convergence conditions, but without a clean notation or an axiomatic treatment.</p>
  <p><strong>The Problem It Solved.</strong> Before Weierstrass, mathematicians relied on vague phrases like "a quantity becomes small" to describe convergence. The absolute value gave a precise language: "$f(x)$ is close to $L$" became $\lvert f(x) - L \rvert < \varepsilon$ — making the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of a limit possible.</p>
  <p><strong>Interesting Fact.</strong> The Triangle Inequality became the defining axiom of a norm when <strong>Stefan Banach</strong> developed his theory of complete normed spaces (now called Banach spaces) in 1922. Every result proved here for $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ on $\mathbb{R}$ generalises directly to norms on infinite-dimensional function spaces — the backbone of modern functional analysis.</p>
  <p><strong>Why It Matters Today.</strong> Every $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ proof in real analysis, every convergence argument in sequences and series, and every continuity proof ultimately reduces to the Triangle Inequality — making it the single most applied inequality in mathematics.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card hindi">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में संक्षेप</div>
  <p><strong>परम मान (Absolute Value)</strong> $\lvert x \rvert$: $x \geq 0$ हो तो $\lvert x \rvert = x$; $x < 0$ हो तो $\lvert x \rvert = -x$। यह $x$ की $0$ से दूरी है। <strong>चार गुण:</strong> (P1) $\lvert x \rvert \geq 0$; (P2) $\lvert x \rvert = 0 \Leftrightarrow x=0$; (P3) $\lvert xy \rvert = \lvert x \rvert \lvert y \rvert$; (P4) <strong>त्रिभुज असमिका</strong> $\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$। <strong>उत्क्रम त्रिभुज असमिका:</strong> $\bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert \leq \lvert x-y \rvert$। दूरी: $d(x,y) = \lvert x-y \rvert$।</p>
</div>

<!-- §4 SOLVED EXAMPLES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x270F;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Solved Examples</h2>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex1">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">1</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Very Easy &nbsp;|&nbsp; Direct Computation</div>
      <div class="example-title">Compute $\lvert -7 \rvert$, $\lvert 3-8 \rvert$, $\lvert (-3)(4) \rvert$, and verify property (P3) for the last</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p>$\lvert -7 \rvert = -(-7) = 7$ (since $-7 < 0$).</p>
    <p>$\lvert 3-8 \rvert = \lvert -5 \rvert = 5$.</p>
    <p>$\lvert (-3)(4) \rvert = \lvert -12 \rvert = 12$.</p>
    <p><strong>Verify (P3):</strong> $\lvert -3 \rvert \cdot \lvert 4 \rvert = 3 \cdot 4 = 12 = \lvert (-3)(4) \rvert$. $\checkmark$ $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex2">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">2</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Easy–Medium &nbsp;|&nbsp; Applying the Triangle Inequality</div>
      <div class="example-title">Given $\lvert x-3 \rvert \leq 1$ and $\lvert y+1 \rvert \leq 2$, find an upper bound for $\lvert (x+y)-2 \rvert$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Rewrite:</strong> $(x+y)-2 = (x-3)+(y+1)$.</p>
    <p><strong>Apply (P4):</strong> $\lvert (x+y)-2 \rvert = \lvert (x-3)+(y+1) \rvert \leq \lvert x-3 \rvert + \lvert y+1 \rvert \leq 1 + 2 = 3$.</p>
    <p>Therefore $\lvert (x+y)-2 \rvert \leq 3$. $\blacksquare$</p>
    <p style="font-size:.88rem;color:#64748b;margin-top:8px;"><em>Key technique: always decompose the expression inside $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ into pieces whose absolute values you know.</em></p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex3">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">3</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Medium–Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; Combining Both Inequalities</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove: $\lvert x^2 - y^2 \rvert \geq \bigl(\lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert\bigr)^2$ for all $x,y \in \mathbb{R}$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Step 1.</strong> Factor: $\lvert x^2-y^2 \rvert = \lvert (x-y)(x+y) \rvert = \lvert x-y \rvert \cdot \lvert x+y \rvert$ by (P3).</p>
    <p><strong>Step 2.</strong> By the Reverse Triangle Inequality: $\lvert x-y \rvert \geq \bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 3.</strong> By the Triangle Inequality: $\lvert x+y \rvert \geq \bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert$ (apply reverse TI to $\lvert x+y \rvert$ and $\lvert -y \rvert$: $\lvert x+y \rvert \geq \lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \rvert$).</p>
    <p><strong>Step 4.</strong> Multiply: $\lvert x^2-y^2 \rvert = \lvert x-y \rvert \cdot \lvert x+y \rvert \geq \bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert^2 = \bigl(\lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr)^2$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex4">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">4</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; CSIR NET / GATE / IIT JAM Level</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove the generalised Triangle Inequality by induction. Deduce a bound when each $\lvert a_k \rvert \leq M$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Claim:</strong> $\displaystyle\Bigl\lvert \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k \Bigr\rvert \leq \sum_{k=1}^{n} \lvert a_k \rvert$ for all $a_1,\ldots,a_n \in \mathbb{R}$, $n \geq 1$.</p>
    <p><strong>Base case $n=1$:</strong> $\lvert a_1 \rvert \leq \lvert a_1 \rvert$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Inductive step:</strong> Assume the inequality holds for $n=m$. Then:</p>
    <p>$$\Bigl\lvert \sum_{k=1}^{m+1} a_k \Bigr\rvert = \Bigl\lvert \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_k + a_{m+1} \Bigr\rvert \leq \Bigl\lvert \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_k \Bigr\rvert + \lvert a_{m+1} \rvert \leq \sum_{k=1}^{m} \lvert a_k \rvert + \lvert a_{m+1} \rvert = \sum_{k=1}^{m+1} \lvert a_k \rvert.$$</p>
    <p>By induction, the inequality holds for all $n \geq 1$. $\square$</p>
    <p><strong>Bound:</strong> If $\lvert a_k \rvert \leq M$ for all $k$, then $\displaystyle\Bigl\lvert \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k \Bigr\rvert \leq \sum_{k=1}^{n} \lvert a_k \rvert \leq \sum_{k=1}^{n} M = nM$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §5 QUICK REVISION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#dbeafe;">&#x26A1;</div>
  <h2>Quick Revision Cards</h2>
</div>

<div class="revision-grid">
  <div class="rev-card a">
    <h4>&#x1F4CA; A — Key Properties &amp; Formulae</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>(P1) $\lvert x \rvert \geq 0$</li>
      <li>(P2) $\lvert x \rvert = 0 \Leftrightarrow x=0$</li>
      <li>(P3) $\lvert xy \rvert = \lvert x \rvert \lvert y \rvert$</li>
      <li>(P4) $\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$</li>
      <li>Rev. TI: $\bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert \leq \lvert x-y \rvert$</li>
      <li>$d(x,y) = \lvert x-y \rvert \geq 0$</li>
      <li>Gen. TI: $\bigl\lvert \sum a_k \bigr\rvert \leq \sum \lvert a_k \rvert$</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card b">
    <h4>&#x2699;&#xFE0F; B — Edge Cases &amp; Conditions</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>$\sqrt{x^2} = \lvert x \rvert$ (NOT $x$)</li>
      <li>$\lvert x+y \rvert = \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$ iff $xy \geq 0$</li>
      <li>$\lvert x \rvert \leq M \Leftrightarrow -M \leq x \leq M$ ($M>0$)</li>
      <li>$\lvert x/y \rvert = \lvert x \rvert / \lvert y \rvert$ for $y \neq 0$</li>
      <li>TI gives upper bound; Rev. TI gives lower bound</li>
      <li>$\lvert x-y \rvert = \lvert y-x \rvert$ (distance symmetric)</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card c">
    <h4>&#x1F3AF; C — Exam Tips</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>&#x1F535; <strong>CSIR NET:</strong> Bound $\lvert f(x) \rvert$: split into pieces, apply TI to each</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E2; <strong>GATE:</strong> Know both forms of Rev. TI separately</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E0; <strong>IIT JAM:</strong> Gen. TI with bound $nM$ — standard estimation technique</li>
      <li>&#x1F534; <strong>B.Sc. Raj.:</strong> Case-split (P3) proof: cover all 4 sign combinations</li>
      <li><strong>All:</strong> $\sqrt{x^2}=\lvert x \rvert$ is a classic MCQ trap</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §6 COMMON MISTAKES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fee2e2;">&#x26A0;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Common Mistakes</h2>
</div>

<div class="warning-box">
  <h4>&#x274C; Errors to Avoid</h4>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 1: Writing $\sqrt{x^2} = x$</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "$\sqrt{x^2} = x$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $\sqrt{x^2} = \lvert x \rvert$. This equals $x$ only when $x \geq 0$. For $x = -3$: $\sqrt{(-3)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3 = \lvert -3 \rvert \neq -3$.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 2: Reversing the Triangle Inequality direction</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "$\lvert x+y \rvert \geq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> The Triangle Inequality gives $\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$ (upper bound). The reverse inequality $\bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert \leq \lvert x-y \rvert$ is a lower bound on $\lvert x-y \rvert$, not on $\lvert x+y \rvert$.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 3: Assuming $\lvert x+y \rvert = \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$ always</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Using equality in the Triangle Inequality without checking. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> Equality holds <strong>if and only if</strong> $xy \geq 0$ (same sign or at least one is zero). Counterexample: $\lvert 1+(-1) \rvert = 0 \neq 2 = \lvert 1 \rvert + \lvert -1 \rvert$.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 4: Omitting cases in the case-split proof of (P3)</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Checking only $x \geq 0, y \geq 0$ and concluding $\lvert xy \rvert = \lvert x \rvert \lvert y \rvert$ always. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> All four cases must be handled: $(+,+)$, $(+,-)$, $(-,+)$, $(-,-)$. Each yields a different expression for $\lvert xy \rvert$ and $\lvert x \rvert \lvert y \rvert$ that must be verified separately.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §7 APPLICATIONS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ecfdf5;">&#x1F310;</div>
  <h2>Real-World Applications</h2>
</div>

<div class="app-grid">
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4F6;</div>
    <h4>Signal Processing</h4>
    <p>Total distortion after $n$ processing stages is bounded by $\sum \lvert e_k \rvert$ — a direct application of the generalised Triangle Inequality for bounding cumulative error.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4BB;</div>
    <h4>Numerical Analysis</h4>
    <p>Each floating-point operation introduces rounding error $\lvert \delta_k \rvert \leq \varepsilon$. The Triangle Inequality gives the absolute bound $\leq n\varepsilon$ after $n$ operations.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4D0;</div>
    <h4>Metric Spaces &amp; Topology</h4>
    <p>$d(x,y) = \lvert x-y \rvert$ makes $\mathbb{R}$ a metric space. The Triangle Inequality for $d$ is exactly (P4). All convergence and continuity definitions on $\mathbb{R}$ use this distance.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4CA;</div>
    <h4>LASSO Regression</h4>
    <p>The $L^1$ norm $\sum \lvert \beta_k \rvert$ is used in LASSO to penalise model complexity. Its non-differentiability at $0$ (unlike $\lvert \cdot \rvert^2$) is what promotes sparse solutions.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §8 SUMMARY TABLE -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#e0e7ff;">&#x1F4CB;</div>
  <h2>Summary Table &amp; Key Result</h2>
</div>

<table class="summary-table">
  <thead>
    <tr><th>Property</th><th>Statement</th><th>Equality condition</th><th>Reference</th></tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td><strong>(P1) Non-neg.</strong></td><td>$\lvert x \rvert \geq 0$</td><td>$\lvert x \rvert = 0 \Leftrightarrow x=0$</td><td>Rudin 1.33</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>(P3) Product</strong></td><td>$\lvert xy \rvert = \lvert x \rvert \lvert y \rvert$</td><td>Always</td><td>Rudin 1.33</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>(P4) Triangle</strong></td><td>$\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$</td><td>iff $xy \geq 0$</td><td>Rudin 1.33</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Reverse TI</strong></td><td>$\bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert \leq \lvert x-y \rvert$</td><td>iff $xy \geq 0$</td><td>Derived</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Gen. TI</strong></td><td>$\bigl\lvert \sum_{k=1}^n a_k \bigr\rvert \leq \sum_{k=1}^n \lvert a_k \rvert$</td><td>By induction</td><td>Example 4</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Distance</strong></td><td>$d(x,y) = \lvert x-y \rvert$</td><td>$d(x,y)=0 \Leftrightarrow x=y$</td><td>Remark</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div class="highlight-result">
  <p><strong>Triangle Inequality:</strong> $\;\lvert x+y \rvert \leq \lvert x \rvert + \lvert y \rvert$</p>
  <p><strong>Reverse Triangle Inequality:</strong> $\;\bigl\lvert \lvert x \rvert - \lvert y \rvert \bigr\rvert \leq \lvert x - y \rvert$</p>
  <p>Both follow from the order structure of $\mathbb{R}$ and are the foundation of every convergence and continuity argument in analysis.</p>
</div>

<!-- §9 CROSS-REFERENCES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x1F517;</div>
  <h2>Cross-References &amp; Related Posts</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F4DA; Prerequisites &amp; Next Steps</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Prerequisites:</strong>
    <a href="/blog/order-relations-and-ordered-sets-english/"><strong>Order Relations and Ordered Sets</strong></a> — $\lvert x \rvert$ is defined using the order on $\mathbb{R}$ &nbsp;|&nbsp;
    <a href="/blog/consequences-of-field-axioms-english/"><strong>Consequences of Field Axioms</strong></a> — sign rules used in proofs &nbsp;|&nbsp;
    <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — case-split and induction proofs.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2192; Next Topic:</strong> <em>Bounded Sets, Supremum and Infimum</em> — absolute values appear throughout LUB arguments and the Archimedean property.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x1F4D6; Further Reading:</strong> Rudin, Ch. 1, §§1.32–1.38; Apostol, Ch. 1, §1.11–1.12; Bartle &amp; Sherbert, Ch. 2, §2.2.</p>
</div>
